skills.teks_description: Write the quadratic function given three specified points in the plane
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced quadratic functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.4(A)). Students should write quadratic functions given three specified points in the plane. Method: substitute each point (x, y) into y = ax^2 + bx + c to create a system of three equations with three unknowns (a, b, c), then solve using elimination, substitution, or matrices. Key concepts: three non-collinear points uniquely determine a parabola, quadratic regression uses technology for best-fit parabolas through data, vertex form reveals maximum/minimum and axis of symmetry, standard form reveals y-intercept. Advanced topics: solving systems involving quadratic equations, analyzing intersections of quadratic and linear functions (0, 1, or 2 solutions), completing the square for any quadratic. Vocabulary: quadratic function, parabola, system of equations, coefficient, standard form, vertex form, factored form, leading coefficient, discriminant, roots, zeros.

skills.teks_description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; determine linear and quadratic factors
skills.prompt: This topic covers polynomial functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.7(B)). Students should add, subtract, and multiply polynomials and determine linear and quadratic factors. Operations: add/subtract by combining like terms; multiply using distributive property or area model. Factoring: find GCF, use grouping, identify special patterns (difference of squares, sum/difference of cubes, perfect square trinomials). Graphs: degree determines maximum number of turning points (n-1) and x-intercepts (n). End behavior: determined by leading coefficient and degree — even degree with positive leading coefficient: both ends up; odd degree with positive leading coefficient: left down, right up. Key concepts: zeros/roots are x-intercepts, multiplicity of zeros affects graph behavior (odd multiplicity crosses, even multiplicity touches), Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (degree n polynomial has exactly n roots counting multiplicity and complex). Vocabulary: polynomial, degree, leading coefficient, end behavior, zero, root, multiplicity, turning point, factor, factor theorem, remainder theorem.

skills.teks_description: Determine asymptotic restrictions and represent domain/range using notation and set theory
skills.prompt: This topic covers rational functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.6(K)). Students should determine asymptotic restrictions and represent domain and range using interval notation and set theory. Rational function: f(x) = p(x)/q(x) where p and q are polynomials. Domain: all real numbers except where denominator equals zero. Vertical asymptotes: occur at x-values where denominator = 0 and numerator is not 0 (function approaches infinity). Horizontal asymptotes: determined by comparing degrees of numerator and denominator — same degree: ratio of leading coefficients; numerator degree less: y = 0; numerator degree greater: no horizontal asymptote (may have oblique). Holes: occur where both numerator and denominator equal zero (common factor). Key concepts: graphing rational functions using transformations of y = 1/x, identifying intercepts, analyzing behavior near asymptotes, representing domain and range using set-builder notation {x | x is not equal to a} and interval notation. Vocabulary: rational function, asymptote, vertical asymptote, horizontal asymptote, oblique asymptote, hole, discontinuity, domain, range, interval notation, set-builder notation.

skills.teks_description: Determine transformation effects on square root functions with parameter variations
skills.prompt: This topic covers radical functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.4(C)). Students should determine transformation effects on square root functions with parameter variations. Parent function: f(x) = sqrt(x); domain x >= 0, range y >= 0; graph starts at origin and increases. Transformations: a*sqrt(x) (vertical stretch/compression and reflection), sqrt(x - h) + k (horizontal shift h, vertical shift k), sqrt(bx) (horizontal stretch/compression). Key features: starting point (vertex) at (h, k) for f(x) = a*sqrt(x - h) + k; domain x >= h; range depends on a and k. Solving radical equations: isolate the radical, square both sides, solve, and always check for extraneous solutions (squaring can introduce invalid answers). Graphing: plot the starting point, use transformations, plot additional points. Key concepts: domain restrictions in radical functions, inverse relationship between quadratic and square root functions, compositions involving radical functions. Vocabulary: radical function, square root, domain, range, transformation, starting point, extraneous solution, inverse function, principal root, index, radicand.

skills.teks_description: Determine transformation effects on exponential and logarithmic graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers exponential and logarithmic functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.5(A)). Students should determine transformation effects on exponential and logarithmic graphs. Exponential parent: f(x) = b^x (b > 0, b is not 1); domain all reals, range y > 0, horizontal asymptote y = 0, passes through (0,1). Logarithmic parent: f(x) = log_b(x); domain x > 0, range all reals, vertical asymptote x = 0, passes through (1,0). These are inverse functions: if y = b^x then x = log_b(y). Transformations: vertical shifts, horizontal shifts, reflections, stretches/compressions follow same rules as other functions. Key features after transformation: shifted asymptotes, changed domain/range, new intercepts. Natural exponential e ≈ 2.718; natural logarithm ln(x) = log_e(x). Common logarithm log(x) = log_10(x). Vocabulary: exponential function, logarithmic function, base, asymptote, inverse function, natural logarithm, common logarithm, transformation, growth, decay, domain, range, e.

skills.teks_description: Rewrite exponential equations as logarithmic equations and vice versa
skills.prompt: This topic covers properties of logarithms for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.5(C)). Students should rewrite exponential equations as logarithmic equations and vice versa. Conversion: b^y = x is equivalent to log_b(x) = y. Properties of logarithms: product rule log_b(MN) = log_b(M) + log_b(N), quotient rule log_b(M/N) = log_b(M) - log_b(N), power rule log_b(M^p) = p × log_b(M). Special values: log_b(1) = 0 (since b^0 = 1), log_b(b) = 1 (since b^1 = b). Change of base formula: log_b(x) = log(x)/log(b) = ln(x)/ln(b), essential for calculator use. Key concepts: logarithms are exponents, using properties to expand or condense logarithmic expressions, solving equations by converting between exponential and logarithmic forms. Applications: pH calculations, Richter scale, decibel scale, doubling time, half-life. Vocabulary: logarithm, base, exponent, product rule, quotient rule, power rule, change of base, common logarithm, natural logarithm, expand, condense, exponential form, logarithmic form.

skills.teks_description: Solve exponential equations of the form y=ab^x where a is a nonzero real number
skills.prompt: This topic covers solving exponential and logarithmic equations for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.5(D)). Students should solve exponential equations of the form y = ab^x. Methods for exponential equations: if bases can be made equal, set exponents equal (same base method); otherwise, take logarithm of both sides and use properties to solve. Methods for logarithmic equations: convert to exponential form, use properties to combine logarithms, then solve. Key steps: isolate the exponential or logarithmic expression, apply appropriate operation (log or exponentiation), solve for variable, check solution (logarithm of negative number or zero is undefined). Applications: compound interest (finding time to reach a target), population growth, radioactive decay (finding half-life or time), cooling/heating curves. Key equations: continuous growth/decay A = Pe^(rt), compound interest A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). Vocabulary: exponential equation, logarithmic equation, isolate, extraneous solution, compound interest, continuous growth, half-life, doubling time.

skills.teks_description: Determine the relationship between the unit circle and the definition of a periodic function
skills.prompt: This topic covers trigonometric functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(A)). Students should understand the relationship between the unit circle and periodic functions. Unit circle: circle with radius 1 centered at origin; point on circle at angle θ has coordinates (cos θ, sin θ). Key angles: 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° and their equivalents in all quadrants; radians conversion: degrees × π/180. Graphs of trig functions: sine and cosine have amplitude 1, period 2π, range [-1,1]; tangent has period π, vertical asymptotes, range all reals. Transformations: y = A sin(B(x - C)) + D where |A| = amplitude, 2π/|B| = period, C = phase shift, D = vertical shift. Key concepts: periodic functions repeat at regular intervals, reference angles, ASTC rule (All Students Take Calculus) for sign of trig functions by quadrant, reciprocal functions (csc, sec, cot). Vocabulary: unit circle, radian, degree, sine, cosine, tangent, period, amplitude, phase shift, vertical shift, periodic function, reference angle, quadrant, reciprocal.

skills.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities including reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, cofunctions, even/odd
skills.prompt: This topic covers trigonometric identities for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(M)). Students should use fundamental trigonometric identities to simplify expressions and verify equations. Reciprocal identities: csc θ = 1/sin θ, sec θ = 1/cos θ, cot θ = 1/tan θ. Quotient identities: tan θ = sin θ/cos θ, cot θ = cos θ/sin θ. Pythagorean identities: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ, 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ. Even-odd identities: cos(-θ) = cos θ (even), sin(-θ) = -sin θ (odd), tan(-θ) = -tan θ (odd). Cofunction identities: sin(π/2 - θ) = cos θ, etc. Strategies for verifying identities: work on one side only (usually the more complex side), convert everything to sine and cosine, factor, find common denominators, multiply by conjugates. Key concepts: an identity is true for all values in the domain, verifying vs. solving equations. Vocabulary: identity, reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean identity, cofunction, even function, odd function, simplify, verify, expression.

skills.teks_description: Generate and solve trigonometric equations in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers solving trigonometric equations for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(N)). Students should generate and solve trigonometric equations in mathematical and real-world problems. Key concepts: solving trig equations is similar to solving algebraic equations but solutions repeat due to periodicity. General solutions include +2πn (or +360°n) for sine and cosine, +πn (or +180°n) for tangent. Methods: isolate the trig function, find reference angle, identify all solutions in [0, 2π), add multiples of the period for general solution. For equations like 2sin²x - 1 = 0: treat as quadratic (let u = sin x), solve for u, then find x. Using identities: substitute using Pythagorean or double-angle identities to get equation in one trig function. Real-world applications: modeling periodic phenomena (tides, daylight hours, sound waves, temperature). Vocabulary: trigonometric equation, general solution, reference angle, period, quadratic form, inverse trig function, principal value, periodic.

skills.teks_description: Apply theorems about angles, radii, chords, tangents, and secants of circles
skills.prompt: This topic covers geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.12(A)). Students should apply theorems about circles including angles, radii, chords, tangents, and secants. Circle theorems: central angle equals intercepted arc; inscribed angle equals half the intercepted arc; tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of tangency; two tangents from external point are equal in length. Chord theorems: perpendicular from center bisects chord; equal chords are equidistant from center. Secant-tangent relationships: power of a point theorems for intersecting chords, secants from external point, and tangent-secant from external point. Arc length: s = rθ (radians) or s = (θ/360)2πr. Sector area: A = ½r²θ (radians) or A = (θ/360)πr². Polygons: sum of interior angles = (n-2)180°; regular polygon interior angle = (n-2)180°/n. Vocabulary: circle, radius, diameter, chord, tangent, secant, arc, central angle, inscribed angle, intercepted arc, sector, segment, polygon, regular polygon, inscribed, circumscribed.

skills.teks_description: Prove triangle congruence using SAS, ASA, SSS, AAS, and HL conditions
skills.prompt: This topic covers proofs in geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.6(B)). Students should prove triangle congruence using SAS, ASA, SSS, AAS, and HL conditions. Triangle congruence postulates/theorems: SSS (three sides equal), SAS (two sides and included angle), ASA (two angles and included side), AAS (two angles and non-included side), HL (hypotenuse-leg for right triangles). NOT valid: SSA/ASS (ambiguous case). Proof formats: two-column proof (statements and reasons), paragraph proof, flow proof. Key proof strategies: mark given information on diagram, identify shared sides or vertical angles, use properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive), apply definitions and postulates. CPCTC: Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent — used after proving triangles congruent to show specific parts are equal. Triangle similarity: AA (two angles), SAS similarity (proportional sides with included angle), SSS similarity (all sides proportional). Vocabulary: congruent, similar, postulate, theorem, proof, corresponding parts, CPCTC, reflexive property, transitive property, included angle, included side, hypotenuse, leg.

skills.teks_description: Derive and use distance, slope, and midpoint formulas to verify geometric properties
skills.prompt: This topic covers coordinate geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.2(B)). Students should derive and use distance, slope, and midpoint formulas to verify geometric properties. Distance formula: d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2), derived from Pythagorean theorem. Midpoint formula: M = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2). Slope: m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1). Applications: prove triangles are isosceles (equal side lengths), right (perpendicular slopes, product = -1), equilateral; prove quadrilaterals are parallelograms (opposite sides equal and parallel), rectangles (parallelogram with right angles), rhombi (parallelogram with equal sides), squares (rectangle and rhombus). Equation of circle: (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2 with center (h,k) and radius r. Proving geometric relationships algebraically using coordinates is called coordinate proof or analytic geometry. Vocabulary: coordinate plane, distance formula, midpoint formula, slope, parallel, perpendicular, coordinate proof, analytic geometry, quadrilateral, diagonal, verify.

skills.teks_description: Use the characteristics of an ellipse to write the equation of an ellipse with center (h,k)
skills.prompt: This topic covers conic sections for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(H)). Students should use characteristics of ellipses to write equations with center (h,k). Conic sections: curves formed by intersecting a cone with a plane. Circle: (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2. Ellipse: (x-h)^2/a^2 + (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1 (horizontal major axis if a > b). Key features of ellipse: center (h,k), vertices (endpoints of major axis), co-vertices (endpoints of minor axis), foci (c^2 = a^2 - b^2). Parabola: (y-k) = a(x-h)^2 or (x-h) = a(y-k)^2; vertex at (h,k), focus, directrix. Hyperbola: (x-h)^2/a^2 - (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1; center, vertices, foci (c^2 = a^2 + b^2), asymptotes. Key concepts: identifying conic type from equation, converting general form to standard form by completing the square, eccentricity. Vocabulary: conic section, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola, circle, center, vertex, focus, directrix, major axis, minor axis, asymptote, eccentricity, standard form.

skills.teks_description: Apply conditional probability in contextual problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers probability for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.13(D)). Students should apply conditional probability in contextual problems. Conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B), the probability of A given B has occurred. Independence: events A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), equivalently P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). Multiplication rule: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A) for dependent events; P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) for independent events. Addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B); for mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). Two-way frequency tables: organize data to calculate marginal, joint, and conditional probabilities. Key concepts: testing independence using probability calculations, Bayes' theorem introduction, tree diagrams for sequential events. Vocabulary: conditional probability, independence, dependent events, mutually exclusive, two-way table, joint probability, marginal probability, tree diagram, sample space, complement.

skills.teks_description: Evaluate the benefits of different sampling techniques including random and convenience sampling
skills.prompt: This topic covers statistics for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.2(A)). Students should evaluate benefits of different sampling techniques. Sampling methods: simple random sample (every member has equal chance), stratified random sample (population divided into subgroups, random sample from each), systematic sample (every nth member), convenience sample (easily accessible, biased), cluster sample (randomly select groups, survey all in chosen groups). Bias: systematic error in sampling; types include response bias, nonresponse bias, selection bias, question wording bias. Distributions: normal distribution (bell-shaped, symmetric, 68-95-99.7 rule), skewed distributions (left or right). Measures: mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation, IQR, z-scores (z = (x - μ)/σ). Sampling distributions: distribution of sample statistics; Central Limit Theorem states sampling distribution of mean approaches normal as n increases. Vocabulary: population, sample, sampling method, bias, random, stratified, systematic, normal distribution, standard deviation, z-score, sampling distribution, Central Limit Theorem, parameter, statistic.

skills.teks_description: Evaluate finite sums and geometric series, when possible, written in sigma notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers sequences and series for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(A)). Students should evaluate finite sums and geometric series written in sigma notation. Sigma notation: Σ represents summation; includes index variable, lower and upper bounds, and expression to sum. Arithmetic series: sum of arithmetic sequence; Sn = n/2 × (a1 + an) = n/2 × (2a1 + (n-1)d). Geometric series: sum of geometric sequence; finite Sn = a1(1 - r^n)/(1 - r) for r not equal 1; infinite S = a1/(1 - r) when |r| < 1 (convergent). Key concepts: writing series in sigma notation, evaluating sums by formula or expanding, recognizing arithmetic vs. geometric patterns, determining convergence of infinite geometric series. Telescoping series: terms cancel leaving only first and last. Applications: compound interest, annuities, bouncing ball distance, repeating decimals as infinite geometric series. Vocabulary: series, sequence, sigma notation, summation, arithmetic series, geometric series, partial sum, convergent, divergent, index, common difference, common ratio, infinite series.

skills.teks_description: Use vectors to model situations involving magnitude and direction
skills.prompt: This topic covers vectors for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(I)). Students should use vectors to model situations involving magnitude and direction. Vector basics: vectors have both magnitude (length) and direction; represented as arrows or ordered pairs <a, b> or in component form ai + bj. Vector operations: addition (tip-to-tail or component-wise), subtraction, scalar multiplication (changes magnitude, may reverse direction). Magnitude: |v| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). Direction angle: θ = tan^(-1)(b/a), adjusted for correct quadrant. Unit vector: vector with magnitude 1; u = v/|v|. Dot product: u · v = |u||v|cos θ = u1v1 + u2v2; result is scalar; used to find angle between vectors. Vectors are perpendicular (orthogonal) when dot product = 0. Applications: force problems (resultant force), velocity (airplane with wind), displacement, navigation. Key concepts: component form, resolving vectors into horizontal and vertical components, equilibrium (sum of forces = zero vector). Vocabulary: vector, scalar, magnitude, direction, component, unit vector, resultant, dot product, orthogonal, equilibrium, displacement, force, velocity.

skills.teks_description: Solve three-variable linear systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution
skills.prompt: This topic covers matrices for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.3(B)). Students should solve three-variable linear systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution. Matrix basics: rectangular array of numbers with rows and columns; dimensions given as m × n. Operations: addition/subtraction (same dimensions, element-wise), scalar multiplication (multiply all elements), matrix multiplication (row × column, resulting dimensions m × p from m×n times n×p). Solving systems with matrices: write augmented matrix [A|b], perform row operations (swap rows, multiply row by nonzero constant, add multiple of one row to another) to reach row echelon form (Gaussian elimination) or reduced row echelon form (Gauss-Jordan). Types of solutions: unique (one solution), no solution (inconsistent, contradictory row), infinitely many (dependent, free variable). Key concepts: determinants, inverse matrices (A^(-1) exists when det(A) is not 0, solution x = A^(-1)b), Cramer's rule. Vocabulary: matrix, dimension, element, entry, augmented matrix, row operation, echelon form, Gaussian elimination, determinant, inverse matrix, identity matrix, singular matrix, system of equations.

skills.teks_description: Add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers
skills.prompt: This topic covers complex numbers for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.7(A)). Students should add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers. Complex number: z = a + bi, where a is the real part, b is the imaginary part, and i = sqrt(-1) so i^2 = -1. Operations: addition (a+bi) + (c+di) = (a+c) + (b+d)i, subtraction similarly, multiplication using FOIL and i^2 = -1: (a+bi)(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i. Complex conjugate: z-bar = a - bi; product z × z-bar = a^2 + b^2 (always real and non-negative). Division: multiply numerator and denominator by conjugate of denominator. Powers of i: cycle every 4 (i^1 = i, i^2 = -1, i^3 = -i, i^4 = 1). Key concepts: complex numbers extend the real number system, every quadratic equation has solutions in the complex numbers (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra), discriminant negative means complex (non-real) solutions in conjugate pairs. Vocabulary: complex number, imaginary number, real part, imaginary part, imaginary unit, complex conjugate, complex plane, modulus, argument, Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

skills.teks_description: Apply volume formulas for three-dimensional figures in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers modeling with geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.11(D)). Students should apply volume formulas for three-dimensional figures in mathematical and real-world problems. Volume formulas: prism V = Bh (B = base area), cylinder V = πr^2h, cone V = (1/3)πr^2h, pyramid V = (1/3)Bh, sphere V = (4/3)πr^3. Surface area formulas: prism SA = 2B + Ph (P = perimeter of base), cylinder SA = 2πr^2 + 2πrh, cone SA = πr^2 + πrl (l = slant height), sphere SA = 4πr^2. Key concepts: choosing appropriate formulas, composite figures (add or subtract volumes), density = mass/volume, optimization problems (maximizing volume for given surface area or minimizing surface area for given volume). Real-world applications: packaging design, storage capacity, construction, liquid capacity. Cross-sections: slicing 3D figures creates 2D shapes; identifying cross-sections of prisms, cylinders, cones, spheres, and pyramids. Vocabulary: volume, surface area, prism, cylinder, cone, pyramid, sphere, base area, lateral area, slant height, cross-section, composite figure, density, capacity.
